sections.content:

Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has 3 source grids (top-left, top-right, bottom-left) with blue/red patterns and a framed target area (bottom-right) with all-blue cells. Center pieces are 6x6 with 2x2 red centers. Clicking center pieces produced no change.
- Goal model: Likely need to select one of the 3 source grids and transfer its pattern into the bottom-right frame, or click on the center of a grid to trigger some transformation.
- Recent findings: Clicking grid centers and frame center had no effect. The board is completely static.
- Plan: Try clicking on individual grid cells (the 6x6 blue/red blocks) to see if they change color. Also try clicking on the frame's corner markers.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 3 of about 43 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Still no change. Let me think about this differently. Maybe I need to click on specific small cells within the center pieces, or maybe I need to click on the grey corner markers of the frame, or the charcoal frame border itself. Let me try clicking on the frame border and corner markers.
Also, I notice the orange bar at the bottom (row 63). Maybe that's interactive too.
Let me try clicking on:
1. The grey corner marker (top-left of frame, around row 32-33, col 32-33)
2. The charcoal frame border
3. The orange bar at the bottom
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the structure. The center pieces have small 2x2 cells within them. Maybe I need to click on those specific small cells.
Looking at the top-left center piece:
- Row 10-11, col 12-13: gg (grey)
- Row 10-11, col 14-15: WW (white)
- Row 10-11, col 16-17: gg (grey)
- Row 12-13, col 12-13: WW (white)
- Row 12-13, col 14-15: RR (red)
- Row 12-13, col 16-17: gg (grey)
- Row 14-15, col 12-13: gg (grey)
- Row 14-15, col 14-17: WWWW (white)
The 2x2 red center is at rows 12-13, cols 14-15. I already tried clicking at (12, 14) which is in that red area.
Let me try clicking on the white cells or grey cells within the center piece. Or maybe I need to click on the frame's corner markers.
Actually, let me try clicking on the grey L-shaped corner markers of the frame. These are at:
- Top-left: around (32, 32) to (35, 35)
- Top-right: around (32, 56) to (35, 59)
- Bottom-left: around (56, 32) to (59, 35)
- Bottom-right: around (56, 56) to (59, 59)
Let me try clicking on one of these.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: 3 clicks so far, all with no board change. Need to try different interactive elements.
Recent findings: Clicking grid cells, center pieces, and frame center all produced no change.
Plan: Try clicking on the grey corner markers of the frame, the charcoal frame border, and the orange bar at the bottom. Also try clicking on the small cells within the center pieces.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Let me try clicking on various key locations systematically
# First, let me look at the grey corner markers more carefully
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Find the corner markers (grey nodes near the frame)
for i, n in enumerate(nodes):
    if n['color'] == 'g' and n['pixels'] == 20:
        print(f"Corner marker node {i}: center=({sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary'])}, {sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary'])}), boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Also look at the frame (charcoal node 34)
frame_node = nodes[34]
print(f"\nFrame node 34: boundary={frame_node['boundary'][:8]}...")

# Try clicking on the top-left corner marker
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 33}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
